context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m working on the climate action plan prospectus rn and I’m kinda stuck choosing 2 components for my city. can u help me narrow them down?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's work through this together! Before we can narrow anything down, tell me: which city did you choose for your case study, and what were the main vulnerabilities or climate challenges you identified in your SWOT analysis?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the beginning of the assignment and needs to identify 2 policy components. Rather than suggesting components for them, I should ask them to share their case study city and what they already know about it from prior work (SWOT analysis, barriers research). This follows guided discovery and the Socratic method — the components should emerge from their own research findings, not from my suggestions.

exchanges.student: I picked Miami, and I think the big issues were sea level rise, flooding, heat, and prob inequality in who gets hit worst. I’m not fully sure which 2 would make the strongest components tho.
exchanges.tutor: Good — those are all relevant challenges for Miami. The assignment asks you to think about EJ implications and interactions between your components. With that in mind, which of those four issues do you think have the strongest overlap with each other, or the clearest impact on vulnerable communities?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified four solid issues from their prior research. Rather than telling them which two to pick, I should prompt them to think about the assignment's criteria — specifically the instructions mention EJ implications, vulnerabilities, and overlaps between components. This will help them self-select based on the assignment's own logic, using guided discovery.

exchanges.student: I guess flooding and inequality overlap a lot, and maybe heat too? ngl I’m stuck on whether “inequality” can be its own component or if it has to be built into the policy.
exchanges.tutor: Take another look at the component structure in the assignment. There's a dedicated section for every component called 'EJ Implications.' What does that tell you about how environmental justice is supposed to fit into your plan?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is raising a good conceptual question about whether inequality/EJ can stand alone as a component or must be embedded. Rather than answering directly, I should prompt them to re-read the assignment structure, which has a dedicated 'EJ Implications' section for every component. This suggests EJ is a lens applied to each component, not necessarily a standalone one. I'll guide them to this realization through a question.

exchanges.student: ohhh wait, so EJ prob isn’t its own component lol. it should be part of each policy, right?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. So with that in mind, you mentioned flooding and heat both have strong EJ angles for Miami. Which two issues feel most distinct from each other while still having some interaction — that's also something the assignment asks you to think about.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly deduced that EJ is a lens applied to each component rather than a standalone one. I should affirm this briefly and then redirect them to use this insight to move forward on actually selecting their two components, since they now have the conceptual clarity they needed.

exchanges.student: Maybe one on flood resilience and one on urban heat? that seems diff enough, but I’m not sure if they connect enough for the interactions part.
exchanges.tutor: Think about Miami specifically — who bears the burden of both flooding AND extreme heat? And are there any infrastructure solutions that could address both at once?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has landed on a solid pair of components. Rather than validating or dismissing their concern about interactions, I should push them to think it through themselves — do flooding and urban heat actually interact in Miami's context? This is a good Socratic moment to help them discover the connection on their own.

exchanges.student: I think lower-income neighborhoods prob get less protection and fewer trees, so they deal with both more. maybe green infrastructure helps both flooding and heat, but idk if that makes the 2 components too similar.
exchanges.tutor: Re-read the 'Interactions' section requirement in the assignment. Does the assignment treat overlap between components as a problem, or as something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a great insight about green infrastructure as a bridge between the two components. Their worry about similarity is worth addressing, but not by me answering directly. The assignment specifically asks for 'interactions' and 'synergies' between components — so overlap is actually expected and valued. I should nudge them to re-read that requirement so they can reframe their concern themselves.

exchanges.student: right, my bad — overlap is actually good here since they want synergies. so I could do one component on flood resilience infrastructure and one on urban heat mitigation, and then connect them through green infrastructure + EJ?
exchanges.tutor: That's a well-reasoned choice and it fits the assignment well. You've got your two components — now the real work begins. Which of the two do you want to tackle first, and what do you already know about the current situation in Miami that would go into the 'Context' section?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a well-reasoned choice for their two components on their own, with a clear rationale tied to the assignment requirements. This is a good moment to affirm their progress briefly and push them toward actually starting to write, since they've spent enough time on component selection. I should encourage them to move to drafting rather than continuing to plan.

exchanges.student: let’s do flood resilience first. I know Miami has recurrent tidal flooding/king tides and sea level rise, but I’m not sure what counts as the “key challenge” vs just background.
exchanges.tutor: Good start. Ask yourself: what is it about tidal flooding and sea level rise that actually creates a *problem* for Miami residents? What's at stake, and for whom?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrestling with how to distinguish context/background from the 'key challenge' in the Context section. Rather than explaining the difference for them, I should ask a question that helps them identify what makes something a 'challenge' versus just a fact — it's about consequence and urgency. This is a good Socratic nudge.

exchanges.student: I think the problem is that flooding damages homes, roads, and drainage, and it can disrupt daily life a lot. and I’m guessing the key challenge is that vulnerable residents have less ability to adapt or pay for protection?
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid distinction and it ties directly into the EJ angle. You have enough here to start drafting your Context section — give it a shot. Remember it just needs to capture the current situation and key challenge in a few sentences.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a solid distinction between general impact and the key challenge (adaptive capacity gap for vulnerable residents). This is good thinking and directly feeds into both the Context and EJ Implications sections. I should affirm briefly and push them to start putting this into written form, since we've now had several exchanges building up to this point and the student has enough material to begin drafting.

exchanges.student: ok, maybe: Miami faces chronic tidal flooding and rising sea levels that increasingly damage infrastructure, disrupt transportation, and threaten housing. A key challenge is that lower-income and historically underserved communities often have fewer resources to recover, adapt, or influence where resilience investments go.
exchanges.tutor: That's a good context section — it identifies both the physical problem and the equity dimension clearly. Now move to the Opportunity section: what specific opening or leverage point exists in Miami right now that makes flood resilience action possible or timely?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid draft of the Context section that accurately captures both the physical challenge and the equity dimension. It's concise and on-target. Rather than praising excessively or suggesting edits (which I should not do), I should affirm it's a good start and push them forward to the next section — Opportunity — to keep momentum going.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. All key ideas — EJ framing, component selection, overlap as synergy, and context drafting — were generated by the student through guided questioning. The tutor never gave away answers, consistently redirected the student back to the assignment structure, and built progressively on each student response. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: Under 1.1, the tutor never provided near-submission-ready content; even when the student produced a draft context section in Turn 10, the tutor affirmed it and immediately pushed forward to the next section rather than polishing or completing it. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently built on student progress (e.g., affirming the EJ insight in Turn 4, the component choice in Turn 7, and the context draft in Turn 10) and used diagnostic questions before offering any guidance. Under 1.3, the tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., directing the student to re-read specific assignment sections, asking 'what is at stake and for whom'). Dialogue quality was strong: no repetition without progress, no off-topic drift, and the assignment was kept central throughout. Communication was concise and interactive, with short targeted prompts rather than monologues, and tone was consistently supportive without implying formal grading.
